| R-01 | Geopolitical / Energy Supply | Risks — More Extensive Conflict-Related Disruptions | No meaningful reopening of the Strait of Hormuz until after 2026Q2, whether due to renewed conflict or logistical challenges. Supply disruptions more severe or prolonged than baseline assumes. | Brent oil price could average $95–$115/bbl in 2026 (vs. $86/bbl baseline); cascading impacts on LNG, fertilizers, food prices | **High** | Monitor Strait of Hormuz shipping volumes weekly; track IEA Oil Market Reports for updated supply data | Page 7, Executive Summary — "Risks of higher commodity prices: More extensive conflict-related disruptions"; Figure 5.A |
| R-02 | Oil Infrastructure | Energy — Oil Risks (Upside) | Re-escalation of hostilities causing additional damage to key oil and gas facilities; global oil supply capacity reduced by up to 4% medium-term relative to pre-war expectations | Severe multi-year oil supply deficit; structural increase in energy costs globally | **High** | Assess pipeline capacity diversification; track alternative egress route utilization (~5.5 mb/d spare pipeline capacity identified) | Page 8, Executive Summary; Page 20, Energy — Oil Risks |
| R-03 | Food Security | Broader Implications — Food Insecurity | Extended conflict keeping oil prices above $100/bbl; knock-on impacts through fuel prices on imported food prices and fertilizer affordability squeeze | Up to 45 million additional people in acute food insecurity in 2026; concentrated in SSA and MNA regions | **High** | Cross-reference with WFP Global Outlook; monitor domestic food price inflation in MNA/SSA economies | Page 10, Executive Summary — "Food insecurity"; Figure 6.D |
| R-04 | Fertilizer Supply | Fertilizers — Key Risks | Longer-than-assumed phase of acute shipping constraints in the Middle East or re-escalation of conflict; China may impose export restrictions on nitrogen fertilizers starting 2026Q2 | Urea prices could exceed 2022 average of $700/mt (the second-highest level in real terms after 1974); severe crop yield reductions globally | **High** | Monitor China customs data on urea/DAP exports; track Qatar production facility damage reports (Ras Laffan) | Page 34, Fertilizers section; Page 35, Figure 18 |
| R-05 | EMDE Economic Growth | Broader Implications — Growth & Inflation | Conflict-related trade disruptions lasting longer than assumed; adverse impacts on EMDE growth, job creation, and development considerably more severe | EMDE commodity exporters grow only 2.4% in 2026 (downgraded 0.9 pp); EMDE inflation could reach 5.3–5.8% under severe disruption scenario | **High** | Monitor World Bank Global Economic Prospects updates; track employment data in commodity-exporting EMDEs | Pages 9–10, Executive Summary — "Growth and inflation prospects in EMDEs"; Figure 6.A–6.C |
| R-06 | Natural Gas Supply | Energy — Natural Gas Risks (Upside) | Further damage to LNG liquefaction plants in the Middle East; longer-than-expected export interruption; no alternative routes exist for Qatari/UAE LNG | Global LNG supply lower than expected; prices could rise far above baseline; EU storage level concerns (at 2022 post-Ukraine-invasion lows) | **High** | Monitor Qatar LNG infrastructure repair timeline; track EU gas storage injection rates (currently at low end of 2017–21 range) | Pages 23, Energy — Natural Gas Risks; Figure 10.C |
| R-07 | Base Metals Supply | Metals — Upside Risks | Export restrictions, new tariffs, or labor/weather/conflict disruptions at critical mines; U.S. Department of Commerce review of copper imports may lead to higher tariffs (expected mid-2026) | Prices for aluminum, copper, tin could surge well above all-time high baselines; market fragmentation | **High** | Monitor U.S. DoC copper import review outcome; track Indonesia nickel ore quota implementation; watch China sulfuric acid export ban (planned May 2026) | Pages 39–40, Metals — Risks; Figure 19.D |
| R-08 | Sulfur Supply Chain | Metals / Agriculture — Cascading Risks | Prolonged decline in sulfur supplies from the Middle East (region accounts for ~25% of global sulfur production); sulfuric acid is critical for leaching copper and nickel ores | Global metals mining broadly challenged; copper and nickel output constrained; Morocco's OCP already cutting phosphate production due to sulfur/ammonia disruptions | **High** | Track sulfur export volumes from Middle East producers; monitor impact on copper/nickel concentrate supply | Page 8, Executive Summary — Risks; Page 40, Metals — Risks; Page 35, Fertilizers (Figure 18.E) |
| R-09 | Precious Metals Volatility | Metals — Precious Metals Risks | Large swings in speculative positioning have amplified precious metals price movements; price forecasts subject to "considerable uncertainty" (document language) | Outsized price swings in gold, silver, platinum; potential for significant price reversal if speculative positions unwind | **Medium** | Monitor ETF inflow/outflow data; track central bank purchasing trends (fell back in 2025 from elevated levels) | Pages 7, 42–43, Metals — Precious Metals Risks; Figure 21.D |
| R-10 | Global Economic Slowdown | Risks of Lower Commodity Prices — Weaker Growth | Unforeseen tightening of global financial conditions; resurgent trade tensions; faltering AI investment returns; China fixed investment remaining soft | Lower-than-projected commodity prices across all groups; metals prices especially sensitive given China's ~50% share of global base metal consumption | **Medium** | Monitor China fixed investment data; track global trade policy uncertainty index (currently elevated, Figure 9.D) | Pages 8–9, Executive Summary — "Weaker-than-expected global economic growth"; Figure 5.D |
| R-11 | Oil Market Surplus (if resolved swiftly) | Risks of Lower Commodity Prices — Ample Supply | If shipping disruptions in the Middle East are swiftly resolved; pre-conflict oil market was on track for ~4 mb/d surplus in 2026 (larger than COVID-19 surplus) | Brent oil could significantly undershoot $86/bbl forecast in 2027; pre-conflict dynamics (sub-$60/bbl) could reassert | **Medium** | Monitor Strait reopening timeline; track OPEC+ production capacity utilization | Page 8, Executive Summary — "Ample oil supply if disruptions are swiftly resolved"; Figure 5.C |
| R-12 | Agricultural / El Niño | Agriculture — Upside Risks | 61% probability of El Niño developing in second half of 2026 (per NOAA); could disrupt coffee, rice, edible oils, sugar, and natural rubber production | Agricultural prices above current forecasts; compounded by already elevated energy and fertilizer costs | **Medium** | Monitor ENSO Index (currently transitioning from La Niña to ENSO-neutral); NOAA updates | Page 30, Agriculture — Risks; Figure 14.C |
| R-13 | Biofuel Demand Escalation | Agriculture — Upside Risks | Higher crude oil prices increasing attractiveness of biofuels; higher biofuel mandates in Indonesia, Thailand, and United States already introduced | Edible oil, sugar, and maize prices rise above projections; sugar price decline reversal possible (especially Brazil ethanol switching) | **Medium** | Track biofuel mandate policy changes; monitor Brazil sugar/ethanol mill switching; follow U.S. biofuel targets | Pages 29–30, Agriculture — "Upside risk: Biofuel demand"; Figure 14.B |
| R-14 | Fertilizer Affordability / Crop Yield | Agriculture — Long-Term Risk | High fertilizer prices forcing farmers to cut application rates or switch to less fertilizer-intensive crops; nitrogen use per hectare in SSA fell 5% and 4% in 2021 and 2022 respectively | Reduced crop yields in future growing seasons; exacerbated food insecurity in low-income, liquidity-constrained farming regions | **Medium** | Monitor fertilizer affordability index trends; track nitrogen application rates in SSA and SAR | Pages 29–30, Agriculture — cost-push risks; Page 31, Figure 15.F |
| R-15 | AI-Driven Power Demand | Energy — Natural Gas / Coal Upside Risks | Faster-than-expected AI adoption increasing data center electricity demand; IEA predicts data centers will account for 8% of global electricity demand growth 2024–2030 | Higher natural gas and coal demand/prices; increased aluminum and copper demand for data center construction | **Medium** | Monitor IEA electricity demand forecasts; track hyperscaler capital expenditure data | Page 23, Natural Gas — "Upside risk: AI-related power demand"; Page 25, Coal Risks; Page 40, Metals Risks |
| R-16 | Indonesia Coal/Nickel Policy | Energy / Metals — Risks | Indonesia lowered nickel ore production quotas for 2026 by ~30%; coal exports from Indonesia expected to decrease under new production targets | Global nickel supply constrained; coal export market tighter; upward pressure on nickel and coal prices | **Low** | Track Indonesian government quota policy implementation; monitor nickel refinery capacity additions | Page 25, Coal — "Downside risk: Surging export supplies from Indonesia"; Page 38, Metals — Nickel |
| R-17 | China Property Sector Weakness | Metals — Downside Risk | Prolonged downturn in China's property sector; subdued construction activity weighing on iron ore, steel, and construction metals demand | Iron ore prices projected to 7-year low; zinc demand dampened; base metals prices could undershoot forecasts | **Low** | Monitor China real estate investment data; track iron ore inventory levels at Chinese ports | Page 39, Metals — Iron ore outlook; Figure 19.A |
| R-18 | EU Natural Gas Storage Shortfall | Energy — Natural Gas Upside | EU gas inventories at low end of 2017–21 range (close to early-2022 levels post-Ukraine invasion); urgent restocking needed during 2026 summer | Competition between East Asian and European LNG buyers → higher-than-anticipated prices in both markets | **Medium** | Monitor AGSI+ EU gas storage data weekly; track Norway pipeline exports and North Africa pipeline flows | Page 22, Energy — Natural Gas "Upside risk: Low storage levels in large economies"; Figure 10.C |
